planting.note: Plant container stock in spring in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral, deep soil in full sun to part shade; Otaheite gooseberry is a Southeast Asian tropical tree producing dense clusters of small, ribbed, waxy, pale-yellow fruits with intensely sour, vitamin-C-rich flesh — they grow directly on the branches and main trunk (cauliflory) and are used throughout Southeast Asia as a sour flavoring in curries, pickles, and relishes.

directions: Plant in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to part shade; tolerates short dry periods once established. Phyllanthus acidus (Otaheite gooseberry or star gooseberry) is a pantropical small tree native to Madagascar and widely grown throughout Southeast Asia, India, and the Caribbean, producing dense clusters of tiny (1/2-inch), deeply ribbed, 6–8 angled, pale cream to yellow fruits that grow directly from the branches and main trunk in a striking display of cauliflory. The fruits are extremely sour and high in vitamin C — almost inedibly tart fresh — but excellent when cooked with sugar to make preserves, pickles, chutney, and a distinctive sour relish used in Filipino and Southeast Asian cooking as a souring agent (similar to tamarind) in soups, stews, and curries. In India, the fruits are pickled in salt and used in rice dishes. The tree is ornamental in its own right — the delicate, deciduous, compound-leafed branches resembling fine fern fronds contrast with the heavy clusters of waxy fruits on the bare branches. In zone 9, the tree may be damaged by hard freezes but usually recovers from the roots. A neglected tropical food tree that produces abundantly with minimal care in warm climates.
sources: Purdue University Center for New Crops — Phyllanthus acidus
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Tropical Fruits
Philippine Department of Agriculture — Kamias/Iba

planting.note: Plant container stock in spring in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, slightly acidic, fertile soil in full sun; palillo is a South American tropical fruit tree in the guava family producing small (1-inch), round to oval, yellow-orange fruits with creamy, sweet-aromatic flesh resembling a cross between guava and mango custard — an important native fruit tree of the Andean and Amazon foothills of Colombia, Ecuador, and Peru.

directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, slightly acidic, fertile soil in full sun; palillo prefers consistent moisture and excellent drainage. Campomanesia lineatifolia (palillo or yellow guabiroba) is a Colombian, Peruvian, and Ecuadoran tropical fruit tree in the myrtle family (Myrtaceae) — the same plant family as guava, feijoa, and jaboticaba — producing small, round to oval, bright-yellow-orange fruits with smooth, thin, aromatic skin and a small quantity of cream to golden, very fragrant, sweet-rich flesh surrounding a few small seeds. The flavor is described as combining guava, mango, and custard in one fruit — aromatic, sweet, with pronounced tropical fragrance. The fruit is eaten fresh or made into juice, ice cream, and preserves; it is an important traditional food of indigenous Andean communities in Colombia and Peru. The genus Campomanesia encompasses many species of native Brazilian and South American myrtle-family trees — some famous (like guabiroba, C. xanthocarpa), most virtually unknown outside their native regions. Palillo is the Colombian and Peruvian name for C. lineatifolia; the tree is an important ornamental in Bogotá urban parks. Extremely rare in cultivation outside South America.
sources: Corporación Colombiana de Investigación Agropecuaria (CORPOICA) — Frutas Amazónicas
Purdue University Center for New Crops — Campomanesia
Jardín Botánico José Celestino Mutis — Bogotá

common_name: Cape Snow Bush (African Rosemary, Wild Rosemary)

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings in spring in very well-drained, lean, slightly alkaline to neutral, gravelly or sandy soil in full sun; Cape snow bush is a South African fynbos shrub with needle-like, intensely aromatic leaves used as a rosemary substitute in cooking and as a traditional Cape Malay spice — it is more drought and heat-tolerant than European rosemary and thrives in hot, dry, calcareous soils.

directions: Plant in very well-drained, lean, slightly alkaline to neutral, gravelly or sandy soil in full sun; prune after flowering to maintain compact shape. Eriocephalus africanus (Cape snow bush or African rosemary) is a native South African fynbos shrub with needle-like, silver-grey, intensely aromatic leaves — the leaves have a fresh, camphor-pine aroma similar to rosemary but with distinctly South African floral-herbal notes. The plant is used in Cape Malay traditional cooking and South African herbal medicine as a seasoning and medicinal herb: the dried leaves flavor lamb stews, braai (barbecue) marinades, and herbal teas; the essential oil is used in aromatherapy for respiratory conditions. Common name 'Cape snow bush' derives from the small, fluffy, white seed heads that cover the plant in late summer, giving a snow-dusted appearance. As a landscape plant, Eriocephalus africanus is outstanding for hot, dry, lean, well-drained soils where European rosemary often struggles with root rot — it has superior drought tolerance and performance in alkaline coastal soils. The cultivar 'Silver Beads' is grown as an ornamental for its exceptionally silvery foliage. An excellent substitute for rosemary in challenging dry garden conditions.
sources: South African National Biodiversity Institute (SANBI)
University of Cape Town — Fynbos Herbs
Botanical Society of South Africa

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in well-drained, lean to moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral, loamy to sandy soil in full sun; Java citronella is the primary commercial source of citronella essential oil worldwide — a large, clumping aromatic grass closely related to lemongrass, grown in Java, Sri Lanka, Taiwan, and Central America for its leaves, which distill to produce the citronella oil used in insect repellents, candles, and soaps.

directions: Plant clump divisions in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; harvest leaves after 4–6 months and steam-distill or use fresh as repellent. Cymbopogon winterianus (Java citronella) is a large, clumping tropical aromatic grass that is the world's primary commercial source of citronella essential oil — one of the most commercially important aromatic crop plants globally, with the highest geraniol and citronellal content of any Cymbopogon species. Java citronella oil (named for its origin in Indonesian cultivation on Java) is the primary active ingredient in citronella candles, insect repellent products, and many natural insect-deterrent sprays worldwide. The plant is named for Cornelius de Winter, a Dutch botanist. Java citronella (C. winterianus) is distinct from Ceylon citronella (C. nardus) — Java citronella has significantly higher citronellal and geraniol content and produces a superior, more commercially valuable oil. The plant resembles lemongrass but has a more pronounced citrus-floral-rose fragrance (from geraniol content) rather than purely lemon. As a garden plant in zone 10–12, Java citronella forms an attractive, 4–6 foot clump of narrow, arching, blue-green leaves with a strong pleasant scent when crushed. Fresh stems placed around outdoor seating areas provide some mosquito deterrence, though less than the concentrated essential oil.
sources: Purdue University — Essential Oil Crops — Cymbopogon winterianus
Indonesian Plantation Research Institute
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Aromatic Herbs

common_name: Giant Chain Fern (Western Chain Fern, Coast Chain Fern)

planting.note: Plant rhizome divisions in spring in moist to wet, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral, fertile soil in full shade to part shade; giant chain fern is one of the largest ferns native to North America, native to moist canyons and stream banks of the Pacific Coast from British Columbia south to Baja California, with fronds reaching 9 feet in optimal conditions — outstanding as a large-scale shade and water feature planting.

directions: Plant in moist to wet, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral soil in deep to part shade; keep consistently moist — does not tolerate drought. Woodwardia fimbriata (giant chain fern or western chain fern) is the largest fern native to North America, growing in the moist, shaded canyons, redwood forests, and coastal creek sides of the Pacific Coast from southern British Columbia south through California to Baja California. In optimal conditions — constantly moist, deeply shaded, humus-rich — the bold, lance-shaped, once-pinnate fronds reach 4–9 feet in length, creating a dramatic, primordial atmosphere in shade gardens. The common name 'chain fern' refers to the chain-like pattern of elongated, linked sori (spore-bearing structures) visible on the underside of fertile fronds — a distinctive feature shared with the eastern Virginia chain fern (W. virginica). Giant chain fern is evergreen in mild climates, semi-evergreen in colder parts of its range. It is an excellent companion for other Pacific Coast woodland natives: sword fern (Polystichum munitum), redwood sorrel (Oxalis oregana), and trilliums. For large-scale shade, stream bank, and bog garden plantings where a dramatic, bold-textured fern is needed. Rarely available commercially but worth seeking from native plant nurseries.
sources: Calflora — Woodwardia fimbriata
University of California Cooperative Extension — Native Ferns
Lady Bird Johnson Wildflower Center

common_name: Sugar Palm (Black Fibre Palm, Aren Palm)

planting.note: Plant container stock in spring in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, deep soil in full sun to part shade; sugar palm is one of Southeast Asia's most economically important palms, tapped for sweet sap that produces palm sugar (aren sugar, gula aren), the distinctly flavored dark-brown sweetener used throughout Indonesian and Filipino cooking — the tapped sap also ferments to produce palm wine (tuak).

directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, deep soil in full sun to part shade; sugar palm tolerates some shade when young. Arenga pinnata (sugar palm or aren palm) is one of Southeast Asia's most culturally and economically important palms, native to tropical Asia from India through Malaysia, Indonesia, and the Philippines, and the primary source of aren sugar (gula aren or gula jawa) — a distinctive, rich, deeply caramel-flavored, dark-brown palm sugar that is the traditional sweetener of Indonesian, Filipino, and Malay cooking. The palm produces sweet sap (neera) by cutting the flower stalks; the fresh sap is collected daily, boiled down to produce palm sugar, or allowed to naturally ferment to produce palm wine (tuak) and then palm vinegar. Beyond sugar, the tree produces fibrous black leaf-base material (ijuk fiber) used for centuries in Indonesia as a natural roofing thatch and rope material — renowned for durability and water resistance. The starchy pith of the trunk trunk is processed into a primitive palm starch (aren starch). The fruits contain three seeds each surrounded by a thick, starchy gel coating that is caustic when raw (irritating calcium oxalate) but edible when processed; young seed gel is used in Southeast Asian desserts (kolang-kaling). A monocarpic palm — it flowers once, over many years, then dies after seed set.
sources: USDA Agricultural Research Service — Arenga pinnata
Indonesian Ministry of Agriculture — Sugar Palm
Purdue University Center for New Crops — Arenga pinnata

planting.note: Plant rooted stem cuttings in spring in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral, moist soil in full sun to part shade; trailing acalypha produces a nearly continuous display of tiny, fluffy, bright-red to magenta caterpillar-like flower spikes on trailing, cascading stems — outstanding in hanging baskets, over walls, and as a colorful tropical groundcover in zone 10–12.

directions: Plant rooted cuttings in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to part shade; pinch stems to encourage branching and more prolific flowering. Acalypha reptans (trailing acalypha or dwarf acalypha) is a small, trailing tropical ornamental perennial in the euphorbia family (Euphorbiaceae), closely related to the larger chenille plant (A. hispida) but compact and trailing rather than erect. The plant produces a nearly continuous display of distinctive, fluffy, caterpillar-like flower spikes (chenille flowers) in bright red to deep magenta-red, 1–2 inches long, dangling from the trailing, cascading stems. The flowers are exclusively ornamental — purely decorative with no food or fragrance value — but are intensely colorful and long-blooming over the warm season. In hanging baskets, trailing acalypha is spectacular — the masses of red caterpillar spikes and the cascading growth habit create an eye-catching display. As a groundcover in zone 10–12 tropical gardens, it spreads rapidly at ground level, providing low color and weed suppression. The plant thrives in the heat and humidity of the Gulf Coast and tropical conditions and blooms most prolifically in bright indirect light or morning sun with afternoon shade. Easily rooted from stem cuttings in water or moist medium.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Acalypha
Louisiana State University AgCenter — Tropical Plants
Missouri Botanical Garden Plant Finder

planting.note: Plant rooted stem cuttings in spring in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, moderately fertile potting mix or garden soil in shade to part shade; Brazilian coleus is a trailing South African plectranthus with distinctive, velvety, rounded, dark-green leaves traced with silver veins on the upper surface and purple on the underside — one of the most beautiful foliage plants for shade containers and hanging baskets.

directions: Plant rooted cuttings in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, moderately fertile soil in shade to part shade; water when the top inch of soil dries. Plectranthus oertendahlii (Brazilian coleus or silver spurflower) is a trailing South African succulent-stemmed perennial in the mint family (Lamiaceae), native to the shaded, moist forest floors of KwaZulu-Natal province in South Africa. Despite the common name 'Brazilian coleus,' it is entirely South African in origin — the misnomer likely arises from its use as a tropical shade plant in Brazilian gardens. The plant produces distinctively beautiful, rounded, scalloped-edged leaves (1–2 inches across) with a velvety texture, dark-green with prominently traced, pale-silver veins on the upper surface and a rich purple-burgundy on the underside — one of the most attractive foliage patterns in the plectranthus family. It produces spikes of small, white to pale-lavender flowers but is grown primarily for foliage. As a shade container and hanging basket plant, P. oertendahlii is outstanding — the trailing stems and beautiful leaf patterning make it an eye-catching specimen. It also works beautifully as a shade terrarium subject and as an indoor plant in low-light situations. The cultivar 'Uvongo' has particularly striking leaf markings.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Plectranthus
South African National Biodiversity Institute (SANBI)
Missouri Botanical Garden Plant Finder
